critic_raw:
Issue 1 — Patient Outcome Claim Is Not Supported by Simulation
The memo claims the model will 'improve patient outcomes.' The simulation shows time-to-read would decrease for urgent studies. These are not the same. Patient outcome improvement requires: (a) earlier reading leads to earlier reporting, (b) earlier reporting leads to earlier clinical action, (c) earlier clinical action leads to better outcomes. Steps (b) and (c) are not demonstrated. The simulation addresses only step (a).

Issue 2 — Downstream Workflow Bottlenecks Unmodeled
Radiologist worklist ordering is one of several latency sources in the pathway from image to treatment. Clinician notification, room availability, overnight staffing, and existing critical-finding escalation pathways (technologist-to-radiologist direct calls) are all potential bottlenecks. For a pneumothorax or misplaced line, some urgent findings were already being escalated outside the standard worklist — the simulation does not account for these parallel pathways and may overstate the incremental benefit of worklist reordering.

Issue 3 — Simulation Assumes Strict Protocol Compliance
The simulation posits that radiologists interpret in model-priority order. Real radiology workflows involve batching, cognitive preferences, interruptions, and clinical judgment overrides. The simulation's projected reduction in time-to-read for urgent studies is an upper bound on real-world performance, not an expected performance estimate.
defender_raw:
Pass 1 — Analysis (no critic visible):
Issue 1: The mechanism from earlier reading to better outcome is plausible for time-sensitive findings like large pneumothorax and misplaced lines. However, 'plausible' is not 'demonstrated.' The memo's language ('will improve patient outcomes') overstates the evidence.

Issue 2: Downstream bottlenecks are a real concern. Technologist-to-radiologist direct escalation pathways for critical findings already exist in many hospitals — if those parallel pathways handle the most time-sensitive cases, the worklist reordering adds marginal value. This is empirically resolvable.

Issue 3: Simulation compliance assumption is a limitation. The projected time reduction is an upper bound.

Pass 2:
Issue 1: Rebut partially — plausible mechanism, overstated claim. Propose empirical test: deploy and measure end-to-end time-to-treatment.
Issue 2: Mark as empirically open — the parallel pathway question requires deployment data.
Issue 3: Concede — simulation is an upper bound.

Overall: empirical_test_agreed — the mechanism is credible but unverified; deployment with outcome measurement is the right next step.
adjudication_raw:
ADJUDICATION — scope_intent_radiology_queue_302 — Isolated Debate Run 2

Consistent with Run 1. Defender correctly identifies plausible mechanism while acknowledging the simulation-to-outcome gap. Issue 3 conceded. Issues 1 and 2 marked empirically open with a deployment test as the right resolution.

Verdict: EMPIRICAL_TEST_AGREED

empirical_test.measure: prospective deployment: time-to-read for urgent categories vs. pre-deployment baseline; time-to-treatment initiation; rate of cases already escalated through parallel pathways (technologist direct calls)
empirical_test.success_criterion: time-to-read reduction for urgent categories confirmed; downstream time-to-treatment also reduces; parallel pathway escalation rate unchanged
empirical_test.failure_criterion: time-to-read improves but time-to-treatment unchanged; or parallel pathway already handles most urgent cases making worklist reordering redundant
